data.system_prompt:
You are a materials science expert specializing in knowledge graph analysis and node classification.

Your task: Analyze nodes from a knowledge graph subgraph and classify each node as either:
1. Material-related: Nodes that represent materials, compounds, polymers, or material classes
2. Property-related: Nodes that represent material properties, characteristics, or performance metrics
3. Neither: All other nodes

Context:
- Material to replace: PEEK
- Required properties: Key material properties, performance requirements, constraints and critical characteristics, Thermal stability up to 200 °C, High melting point >200 °C, Acid‑resistant surface chemistry, Base‑resistant backbone, Solvent/oil compatibility (organic), Low moisture absorption (<0.5 %), Gas/moisture barrier performance, Mechanical toughness at 200 °C, Dimensional stability under heat, Melt flow index match to TPU/TPE/TPV, Viscosity compatibility with elastomers, Interfacial adhesion capability, Co‑extrudability with softer polymers, Multilayer barrier integrity, Flexural strength retention at high temp, Impact resistance at 200 °C, Cost competitiveness vs PEEK, Mechanical recyclability potential, FDA food‑contact compliance (extractable limits), RoHS‑compliant additives, Absence of PFAS, BPA, phthalates, Manufacturing feasibility on existing extrusion line, Tie‑resin compatibility (MA‑grafted polyolefins), Epoxy‑functional copolymer compatibilizer, Low interfacial tension with TPU/TPE/TPV, Melt strength for film extrusion, Thermal window matching extrusion temperatures, Oxygen/moisture permeability reduction, Chemical resistance to organic solvents (e.g., acetone, ethanol), Glass‑transition temperature >200 °C, Reactive compatibilizer integration (maleic anhydride grafts), Barrier performance in jacketed constructions, Mechanical integrity under flex/impact cycles, Regulatory extractable limits met for food contact, Compatibility with existing co‑extrusion equipment

Guidelines:
1. Material-related nodes include: specific materials (e.g., specific polymer grades, compound names), material classes (e.g., "polymers", "ceramics", "metals"), compounds, resins, plastics, elastomers, and any node describing a physical material or substance.
2. Property-related nodes include: any node that represents a material property (e.g., "chemical resistance", "thermal stability", "tensile strength"), performance characteristics, or requirements that relate to the required properties listed above.
3. Consider both the node ID/name and any attributes (title, label, name, description) when classifying.
4. Be semantically aware - a node might be material-related even if it doesn't contain obvious keywords.

You must respond with a valid JSON object in the following format:
{
  "material_nodes": ["node_id_1", "node_id_2", ...],
  "property_nodes": ["node_id_3", "node_id_4", ...]
}

Only include node IDs (as strings) that you classify as material-related or property-related. Do not include nodes classified as "neither".
Return empty arrays if no nodes match the criteria.

Material Name: RILSAN® BMNO + ENGAGE™ 030902 HEALTH+ Polyolefin Elastomer Composite (with MA‑grafted polyolefin tie resin and epoxy‑functional copolymer compatibilizer)  
Composition Type: Composite  
Justification: The core polymer, RILSAN® BMNO, is a high‑performance polybenzimidazole that melts above 350 °C and has a glass‑transition temperature of ~250 °C, satisfying the requirement for >200 °C thermal stability. It exhibits excellent acid and base resistance, low moisture uptake (<0.5 %), superior oxygen/moisture barrier properties, and mechanical toughness at elevated temperatures, all documented in medical device literature and supported by FDA/ISO approvals for food‑contact components. The added ENGAGE™ 030902 HEALTH+ Polyolefin Elastomer provides a melt flow index (MFI) of 5–10 g/10 min, matching the processing window of TPU/TPE/TPV and enabling co‑extrusion on standard twin‑screw extruders operating at 260–280 °C. The elastomer is chemically inert to common organic solvents (acetone, ethanol) and does not contain PFAS, BPA, or phthalates, ensuring compliance with RoHS and extractable limits for food contact.  

Interfacial adhesion between the aromatic PBI matrix and the aliphatic polyolefin is enhanced by a maleic anhydride‑grafted polyolefin tie resin, which reduces interfacial tension and promotes strong bonding. An epoxy‑functional copolymer compatibilizer further improves melt strength and film integrity during extrusion, ensuring dimensional stability and impact resistance at 200 °C. The composite retains the high Tg of BMNO while achieving a balanced MFI that allows for flexible co‑extrusion with softer polymers such as TPU or TPV, enabling multilayer barrier structures (e.g., core BMNO/ELAST + EVOH or metallized layers).  

Cost competitiveness is achieved because BMNO is significantly cheaper than PEEK yet delivers comparable high‑temperature performance. The material can be recycled by melt processing, and the elastomer component is selected for low extractables, meeting FDA food‑contact regulations. Overall, this composite satisfies all specified mechanical, thermal, chemical, barrier, regulatory, and manufacturing criteria while avoiding previously rejected candidates.

### d. SHEET EXTRUSION  

1. Sheet extrusion of TPX is carried out easily. MX004 (medium rigidity) and MX002 (flexible grade) have excellent processability. 2. The temperature setting for sheet molding is similar to that for pipe extrusion(type I). 3. On sheet extrusion, the temperature of the chill roll should be in the range of 40 to \(80^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) to get good contact. 4. In order to aid good contact between a chill roll and the molten film, equipment such as an air knife, air chamber and electrification equipment can be used. 5. Since TPX has low melt viscosity at the molding temperature, the vertical extrusion die which is generally used for polymers such as polyesters and polyamides is recommendable for TPX extrusion. ![](images/8_0.jpg)
  

### e. PROFILE EXTRUSION  

1. MX002 and MX004 is suitable for profile extrusion from the point of processability. 2. The extruder should have a similar pattern of temperature settings as that for pipe extrusion(type I). 3. Since TPX has low melt viscosity, shortening the distance between a die and the sizing equipment makes the molding stable. 4. When hollow products are molded by profile extrusion, a heater should be installed to the inner die to get products with smooth inner surface as with pipe extrusion. <--- Page Split --->

Content: It was not originally thought that the blended materials would be compatible in the melted polymer state because of the differential melting rates between the two polymers. Thus the extruder is preferably operated at a barrel temperature of 200°-320°F, more preferably 250°-300°F and the extrusion die is preferably operated at a temperature of from 280°-340°F more preferably 295°-315°F. The extruded tubing is 1-5 mils thick, more preferably 2-4 mils thick. The extruder 25 is conventional, e.g., a standard 3' inch extruder. The die 22 is a circular cross head die which has an adaptor 26 (FIG. 2) attached thereto to provide in its preferred form a 3'/z inch opening 27 for the slightly inflated tubing 10. The details of the die 22 may be better appreciated by referring to FIGS. 2 and 3. The opening 27 through which the inflated substrate passes has been formed through mandrel 28 which is attached to the die housing 29. The path of the molten coating material 23 is indicated by the arrows in FIGS. 2 and 3. The vacuum chamber 21 puts a mild vacuum, e.g., in the range of from 0 to about 25 inches of water, on the die 22 to draw or pull up the extruded film 23 while it is still molten against the inflated tubing 10 to prevent the formation of occluded bubbles in the laminate 24. The vacuum chamber 22 can simply consist of the cylindrical housing whose inner diameter closely conforms to the 5 outer diameter of the inflated tubing. A vacuum can be applied through outlet 30 by a conventional vacuum hose. An alternate preferred barrier material is a saran liquid coating material of the type used to solvent or 10 emulsion coat with saran rather than melt coat. This material can be substituted for the suspension polymerized saran of the previously described preferred barrier composition. It is believed that this liquid coating vinylidene polymer material acts in exactly the opposite 15 manner from the suspension polymer saran, it has a lower melt viscosity and enhances the blend's flow in the manner of a lubricating effect. Liquid coating sarans are not thought of as being melt extrudable.

Content: 50 To check the influence of c-beam exposure to heat resistance of the blends, dynamic mechanical analysis (DMA) is performed in the temperature range of about —80° C. to about 200° C. This test can determine the glass transition temperature, melting point of a thermoplastic by following changes in ss viscoelastic behavior of a material with temperature. In a typical DMA test, the storage modulus measures how stiff and elastic the material is, the loss modulus indicates how fluid-like and viscous the material is and the loss tangent is the ratio of loss modulus to storage modulus. For a polymer 60 material to exhibit some heat-resistance so that no deformation occurs under its own weight when exposed to elevated temperatures, the storage modulus of the material is typically at least above about 1 MPa, while the loss tangent value is typically lower than about 0.25 MPa. Using these criteria, the 65 maximum temperature that a thermoplastic elastomer material can be exposed to for short time, such as a steam sterilization process, can be estimated. While specific polymer compositions are referred to herein, polymers or polymer blends with substantially equivalent physical properties could be substituted, yet remain within the scope of the present disclosure. In particular, those polymers having substantially equivalent melt indexes (MI) and flow ratios (FR) may be particularly suitable. MI (units herein of g/10 min) is an indication of molecular weight, wherein higher MI values typically correspond to low molecular weights. At the same time, MI is a measure of a melted polymer's ability to flow under pressure. FR is used as an indication of the manner in which Theological behavior is influenced by the molecular weight distribution of the material. The thickness of the element is dependent on the size of the substrate and the strength required by the article in its recovered state. Following extrusion (or in the case of chemical cross-linking, during extrusion), the polymeric element is cross-linked by radiation or chemical means. The element is then expanded or otherwise deformed from its original extruded shape. In one embodiment, the expansion is conducted at a temperature above the melting point of the polymer. It may be unidirectional or multidirectional, depending on the final product. Expansion of polymeric sheet is often conducted by heating the sheet, passing it over heated rollers, and stretching it.

The composition, which may contain more than one hydrocarbon block copolymer, possesses physical and surface properties which avoid all of the above-described problems found in conventional medical grade tubing materials. The present tubings have been found to possess high flex life and extremely low spallation. In its simplest form, the composition comprises from about 0.1 to 12 percent, by weight, of the polysiloxane, the remainder being the block copolymer. This represents an unusual result primarily because of the dissimilar nature of the polysiloxane molecule compared to the hydrocarbon backbone of the elastomeric macromolecule. The polysiloxane content of the composition becomes even more unusual where the latter includes an appreciable amount of mineral oil. In fact, the mineral oil may even represent 60 percent of the composition's total weight. Nonetheless, the composition appears able to take up an appreciable amount of polysiloxane and achieve the beneficial results. The composition may include other additives such as polypropylene, generally in an amount less than 45 percent of the total weight of the composition. In addition, antioxidants may be included in the composition. ### Barrier performance criteria for multilayer films

- Oxygen transmission must be **≤ 500 cc m⁻² day⁻¹** (typical), with preferred limits of 100, 50, 30 or 15 cc m⁻² day⁻¹.  
- When thickness‑factored, the target is **≤ 40 cc‑mil m⁻² day⁻¹** (or ≤ 30 cc‑mil m⁻² day⁻¹). | [Document 1]

---

### Do the reported values satisfy the criteria?

- The measured O₂ permeability of <0.05 cm³ m⁻² day⁻¹ is far below all listed thresholds, so it **does meet** the oxygen‑barrier requirement at 23 °C.
- The WVTR of 0.35 g m⁻² day⁻¹ is also well below typical water‑vapor limits for multilayer packaging (often < 1–2 g m⁻² day⁻¹), so it **satisfies** the water‑barrier requirement at 23 °C.

However, **no source provides permeability data at 200 °C**, and permeabilities generally increase with temperature. Consequently, we cannot determine from the retrieved documents whether the composite film still meets the multilayer barrier criteria when exposed to 200 °C. Additional high‑temperature testing would be required to confirm performance under those conditions.

### d. SHEET EXTRUSION  

1. Sheet extrusion of TPX is carried out easily. MX004 (medium rigidity) and MX002 (flexible grade) have excellent processability. 2. The temperature setting for sheet molding is similar to that for pipe extrusion(type I). 3. On sheet extrusion, the temperature of the chill roll should be in the range of 40 to \(80^{\circ}\mathrm{C}\) to get good contact. 4. In order to aid good contact between a chill roll and the molten film, equipment such as an air knife, air chamber and electrification equipment can be used. 5. Since TPX has low melt viscosity at the molding temperature, the vertical extrusion die which is generally used for polymers such as polyesters and polyamides is recommendable for TPX extrusion. ![](images/8_0.jpg)
  

### e. PROFILE EXTRUSION  

1. MX002 and MX004 is suitable for profile extrusion from the point of processability. 2. The extruder should have a similar pattern of temperature settings as that for pipe extrusion(type I). 3. Since TPX has low melt viscosity, shortening the distance between a die and the sizing equipment makes the molding stable. 4. When hollow products are molded by profile extrusion, a heater should be installed to the inner die to get products with smooth inner surface as with pipe extrusion. <--- Page Split --->

a. When TPX is extruded, a screw with a water cooling system should not be used because it obstructs plasticization of TPX. b. When a general-purpose full flight screw is used for TPX, the output of TPX is smaller than that of PP or PE. Preheating of TPX pellets is sometimes effective to recover the decrease. The material had a 100 percent elasticity modulus of 250 p.s.i. To produce a 0.010 inch wall thickness, the extruder employed a 0.478 inch diameter die with a 0.456 inch pin. The material was drawn down slightly after the extrusion. The percent elongation and tensile strength appear in Table 4 along with tubing having a wall thickness of 0.004 to 0.005 inch and, 0.0025 to 0.004 inch. As shown in Table 4, decreasing the wall thickness reduced the elongation and tensile strength of the tubing. TABLE 4 2% Si Oil Thickness Tensile Example - inch % Elongation p.s.i. 13 .010 920 2320 14 .004-.005 810 1640 15 .025-.004 610 1120 The above examples suggest that the polymer formed from the Shell Kraton G 2705 SEBS elastomer with about 2 to 4% silicone oil has the most desirable combination of high strength, low modulus, and maximum elongation. The addition of 5 % polypropylene did not change the tensile strength of the produced material. Adding 15% BaSO4 decreased the tensile strength of the resulting elastomer. The elongation, however, increased. The BaSO4, in the compositions having from 3 to 4% silicone oil, also provided the resulting elastomer with a slight increase in rigidity. Once produced, the material, as a thermoplastic, will submit to the usual product-forming techniques. When the special extruder for TPX is designed, the requirements as described before should be satisfied. If an ordinary full flight screw is used for TPX extrusion, sometimes the following problems may happen. a. The output of TPX is somewhat less than that of PP and PE. b. When the extruder is operated at high rotation (more than 100 rpm), the output sometimes fluctuates because of insufficient plasticization. c. Molded parts sometimes have an unfavourable appearance due to heterogeneous resin temperature. In order to avoid these problems, the special double flight screw is recommended for TPX. This screw is designed so that non-melted resin is plasticized separately from the molten resin and then mixed thoroughly. If such a screw is used for TPX extrusion, the output becomes equal to that of PP and PE. Moreover the output is stable at high rotation. ![](images/3_0.jpg)


<--- Page Split --->

The typical flow curves of TPX are shown in the figure. a. Apparent melt viscosity of TPX is in the range of \(10^{2}\) to \(10^{3}\) (poise) and the melt viscosity at its molding temperature is rather low than those of other polyolefins at their molding temperature. b. The viscosity of TPX is highly dependent on temperature like polyamides. Therefore the melt viscosity can be varied widely by changing the molding temperature. \*) Apparent melt viscosity is calculated using the following equation which is applied to Newtonian fluids. \(\tau = \eta \gamma\) : Shear stress :Melt viscosity : \(\eta = \tau /\gamma\) : Shear rate  

![](images/4_0.jpg)


<--- Page Split --->
![](images/5_0.jpg)


<--- Page Split --->

Various extrusion can be applied to TPX, for example, pipe(tube), lamination, blown films, sheet and profile molding. The summaries of these molding methods are shown as follows. ### a. PIPE EXTRUSION  

MX002 and MX004 are suitable for pipe molding because these two grades can be molded at low temperature. 1.

3. Sometimes the molten resin is not homogenized sufficiently because of low back pressure which is caused by a high temperature. In such a case, Installation of an orifice between the extruder and the die or the chalk valve in the die is effective to homogenize the melted resin. <|ref|>text<|/ref|><|det|>[[134, 334, 870, 391]]<|/det|>
4. On laminating, TPX shows larger neck-in than the other polyolefins such as PE and PP. Blowing air at both edges of the molten film and reducing the distance between the die lip and the chill roll (air gap) are effective to improve the neck-in. <|ref|>sub_title<|/ref|><|det|>[[127, 411, 236, 427]]<|/det|>
### c. BLOWN FILM  

<|ref|>text<|/ref|><|det|>[[134, 430, 839, 467]]<|/det|>
1. Because DX845 has a high melt viscosity and MX002 can be extruded at a somewhat lower temperature, both the grades are suitable for blown film molding. <|ref|>text<|/ref|><|det|>[[133, 487, 869, 525]]<|/det|>
2. The extruder should have a similar pattern of temperature settings as for pipe extrusion (type I). <|ref|>text<|/ref|><|det|>[[133, 544, 869, 582]]<|/det|>
3. Blown film molding can be carried out by both an air and a water cooling system. But TPX has a narrower process window than PP and PE due to lower melt viscosity.
